sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Vertigo is a sensation of movement, often described as spinning or a feeling of being pulled in one direction, even when you are standing still. Vertigo is a symptom, not a diagnosis, and results from disturbances in the vestibular system. Vertigo is categorised into peripheral (inner ear or vestibular nerve involvement) and central (brainstem or cerebellum involvement). The main causes are benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV), vestibular neuritis and cerebrovascular accidents (CVA)

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Vertigo Peripheral Central
- Spinning sensation (self or environment)
- Worsened by head movement
- Nausea/Vomiting
- Unsteadiness/gait disturbance
- Sudden onset
- Severe intensity
- Positional trigger (e.g., rolling over in bed)
- Horizontal nystagmus
- Hearing loss/tinnitus
- No neurological deficits
- Abnormal head impulse
- Gradual or abrupt onset
- Mild or constant intensity
- Focal neurological signs (e.g., diplopia, dysarthria, ataxia)
- Vertical or directional changing nystagmus
- No hearing involvement
- Unstable gait or inability to walk
- Slurred speech and double vision (diplopia)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Sudden severe vertigo with neurological signs, inability to walk, new headache, neck pain, or reduced conscious state should be treated as possible central vertigo or stroke
- Hearing loss, tinnitus, or ear symptoms can support a peripheral cause, but sudden hearing loss in acute vestibular syndrome can also occur with posterior circulation stroke
- Brief movement-triggered symptoms lasting less than 2 minutes are more consistent with BPPV than with vestibular neuritis or stroke
- HINTS and positional manoeuvres should only be used by clinicians trained in their limitations and only when the presentation fits acute vestibular syndrome or BPPV

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position for comfort and safety, particularly if vomiting or unsteadiness is present
- Record vital signs, including blood glucose and postural observations where appropriate
- Perform focused neurological assessment, including FAST where appropriate, and assess gait if safe
- Check for nystagmus and associated ear or hearing symptoms
- Consider HINTS only in acute vestibular syndrome and only if specifically trained in its use
- Consider Dix-Hallpike or Epley manoeuvre only when the presentation is strongly consistent with BPPV and contraindications have been excluded

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG if the diagnosis is uncertain or a cardiac or syncopal cause remains possible
- Consider vascular access if persistent vomiting, dehydration, or central pathology is suspected
- Consider IV fluids only where clinically indicated
- Consider antiemetic therapy where vomiting is prominent and serious central pathology has been considered
- Consider additional vestibular symptom treatment only in line with local protocol and without delaying transport when red flags are present

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Patient education: advise around positional triggers and safety measures such as driving, working at heights, and operating machinery during episodes
- HINTS is an expert bedside examination for acute vestibular syndrome and should not be used in isolation by untrained clinicians to rule out stroke
- If examination is central or equivocal, or if there are additional neurological red flags, treat as possible central vertigo and escalate urgently
